chunk_summary: Post-discharge, preterm/VLBW infants are at risk of calcium and phosphorus deficiency. ESPGHAN and Indian guidelines recommend specific supplementation targets, monitoring biochemical markers, and safe mineral delivery strategies to prevent metabolic bone disease.
learning_objectives: Understand the risk factors for calcium and phosphorus deficiency in preterm/VLBW infants after discharge.
Learn ESPGHAN and Indian guidelines for calcium and phosphorus supplementation in preterm infants.
Identify biochemical markers and imaging strategies for metabolic bone disease diagnosis.
Educate families on safe mineral delivery methods and the importance of follow-up.
key_facts: Fetal mineral accretion peaks in late gestation, increasing deficiency risk in preterm infants.
ESPGHAN recommends calcium intake of 120–200 mg/kg/day and phosphorus intake of 70–115 mg/kg/day for enterally fed preterm infants.
Indian guidelines suggest calcium supplementation of 120–140 mg/kg/day and phosphorus 60–90 mg/kg/day for VLBW infants on exclusive human milk until term-equivalent age.
High ALP with low serum phosphorus is a typical biochemical pattern in metabolic bone disease.
Safe mineral delivery requires measured fortification or prescribed supplements, not unmeasured home additives.
reference_quote: ESPGHAN provides practical target ranges of calcium about 120–200 mg/kg/day and phosphorus about 70–115 mg/kg/day for enterally fed preterm infants.
document_key_topics: Calcium and phosphorus supplementation in preterm infants
Biochemical monitoring for metabolic bone disease
